responses.strategic_rationale: The project balances high‑resolution longitudinal data capture against tight budgets, staffing constraints, and participant safety. Levers focus on recruitment breadth, staffing flexibility, annotation efficiency, safety infrastructure, data governance, and fiscal prudence. Together they address core tensions between speed of enrollment, data quality, operational cost, and regulatory compliance, ensuring the unit can meet its 36‑month success criteria while remaining adaptable to unforeseen challenges.

levers.name: Recruitment Channel Expansion
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever broadens the pool of potential participants, accelerating enrollment but potentially diluting participant quality due to broader screening criteria. The increased volume may also strain pre‑admission screening resources.
options: Launch a national outreach campaign to recruit from sleep clinics across Germany and surrounding regions.
Establish an online pre‑screening portal to identify potential participants before in‑person visits and reduce initial screening time.
Provide telehealth screening sessions for participants to reduce travel burden and increase enrollment flexibility and engagement.
levers.review_lever: Expanding recruitment channels increases enrollment speed, but the options overlook the risk of diluting participant quality due to broader screening criteria.

levers.name: Staffing Schedule Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever improves technician coverage during peak event times, potentially reducing overtime costs but may increase fatigue risk if shifts are too tightly packed. It also affects staff morale and retention.
options: Implement a rotating shift system that aligns technician coverage with peak event times to optimize response.
Introduce part‑time night technicians to supplement full‑time staff during high‑demand periods and reduce overtime costs.
Adopt a cross‑training program enabling technicians to perform both monitoring and basic scoring tasks and improve workflow.
levers.review_lever: Optimizing staffing schedules improves coverage, yet the options ignore the potential for technician fatigue during consecutive night shifts.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Automation
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces manual annotation time, increasing throughput but may introduce false positives if algorithmic thresholds are not carefully tuned. Continuous human oversight remains essential to maintain data integrity.
options: Deploy a rule‑based event detection algorithm to flag potential parasomnia episodes for review and reduce manual load.
Integrate a semi‑supervised learning model that learns from technician annotations to suggest event boundaries and improve consistency.
Use a consensus scoring interface that aggregates multiple technician inputs to streamline final adjudication and reduce errors.
levers.review_lever: Automating annotation reduces manual workload, but the options fail to address the need for continuous human oversight to validate algorithm outputs.

levers.name: Safety Monitoring Enhancement
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases participant protection, potentially lowering incident rates but adds complexity to the nightly workflow. It may also require additional training for staff.
options: Install real‑time motion sensors that trigger alerts for sudden movements during sleep and notify staff immediately.
Set up a nightly safety check protocol where technicians verify participant status before bed and document findings.
Equip suites with emergency call buttons linked to central monitoring station and provide training on their use.
levers.review_lever: Enhancing safety monitoring boosts participant protection, yet the options do not consider the added complexity of integrating alerts into existing workflows.

levers.name: Data Sharing Governance
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever protects privacy and ensures compliance, but may slow down data access for external researchers. It also requires ongoing governance oversight.
options: Create a de‑identification pipeline that removes all video frames before public release and verifies data integrity.
Implement a controlled‑access repository requiring data use agreements for all users and enforce periodic audits.
Establish a data governance board to review each sharing request for compliance and maintain audit trails.
levers.review_lever: Refining data sharing policy protects privacy, but the options miss the challenge of ensuring timely access for legitimate researchers.

levers.name: Budget Contingency Management
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever safeguards against overruns, but may reduce flexibility for urgent equipment upgrades or unexpected staffing needs. It also requires disciplined financial monitoring.
options: Allocate a fixed contingency reserve of 10% of total budget for unforeseen expenses and monitor usage.
Set up a phased spending schedule tied to milestone completion to monitor cash flow and adjust allocations.
Negotiate flexible payment terms with equipment vendors to defer costs until deployment and reduce upfront capital.
levers.review_lever: Implementing budget contingencies safeguards finances, but the options overlook the risk of over‑conservatism stalling necessary equipment upgrades.

responses.strategic_rationale: Strategic Rationale: The Bonn parasomnia unit must balance rigorous data capture with participant safety, operational feasibility, and regulatory compliance while staying within a tight budget. Core tensions arise between maximizing event yield and minimizing participant burden, between expanding capacity and preserving data integrity, and between rapid protocol iteration and ethical oversight. The selected levers target these conflicts by tightening environmental controls, standardizing equipment, accelerating ethics workflows, safeguarding data, preparing for scalability, and fostering interdisciplinary collaboration. Together they create a resilient framework that aligns scientific rigor with practical constraints, ensuring the unit can deliver high‑quality longitudinal data, meet publication milestones, and position itself for future expansion.

levers.name: Participant Experience Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will improve participant comfort and adherence, potentially increasing event capture rates. However, higher comfort measures may require additional resources and could introduce variability in environmental conditions that affect data quality.
options: Implement a personalized sleep environment customization protocol that allows participants to adjust lighting, temperature, and bedding within safety limits.
Implement a real‑time participant feedback loop using unobtrusive questionnaires to capture comfort perceptions and adjust conditions accordingly.
Implement a rotating volunteer support schedule that provides participants with a familiar face during nights to reduce anxiety.
levers.review_lever: Balancing participant comfort with data integrity; the options focus on environmental adjustments but ignore potential confounding introduced by variable lighting or temperature on sensor signal quality.

levers.name: Equipment Calibration Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will standardize sensor outputs, improving event detection accuracy and reducing false positives. However, frequent calibration may interrupt nightly monitoring and increase technician workload.
options: Implement a nightly pre‑sleep calibration routine that verifies dry‑electrode EEG impedance and mattress sensor thresholds before participant arrival.
Implement a quarterly cross‑suite sensor drift audit that compares recorded baseline signals against a reference standard.
Implement an automated calibration alert system that notifies technicians when sensor performance deviates beyond acceptable thresholds.
levers.review_lever: Ensuring calibration consistency is critical, yet the options overlook the trade‑off between calibration frequency and uninterrupted data collection.

levers.name: Ethics Compliance Cadence
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will streamline ethics approvals and participant consent processes, reducing administrative delays. However, a faster cadence may risk overlooking nuanced consent issues or emerging regulatory changes.
options: Implement a rolling ethics review schedule that submits protocol amendments to the institutional review board on a monthly basis.
Implement a real‑time consent monitoring dashboard that tracks participant understanding and flags potential comprehension gaps.
Implement a stakeholder liaison role that maintains continuous dialogue with regulatory bodies to anticipate policy shifts.
levers.review_lever: Accelerating ethics processes can reduce delays, but the options fail to address the need for adaptive consent models that evolve with participant experience.

levers.name: Data Integrity Assurance
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will enhance data quality, ensuring reliable downstream analyses and publication credibility. However, additional integrity checks may increase storage demands and processing time.
options: Implement a nightly checksum verification step that cross‑checks raw sensor files against the master archive before backup.
Implement a versioned data tagging system that records acquisition parameters and software versions for every recording session.
Implement a redundancy protocol that mirrors critical data streams to a secondary NAS within the same building to guard against hardware failure.
levers.review_lever: Maintaining data integrity is essential, yet the options do not consider the impact of increased storage costs on the overall budget.

levers.name: Operational Scalability Planning
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will prepare the unit for future expansion, ensuring that staffing, equipment, and protocols can grow without compromising quality. However, early scalability planning may divert resources from immediate operational needs.
options: Implement a modular suite design that allows rapid addition of two new rooms without major renovation.
Implement a scalable staffing model that trains technicians to cover both standard and enhanced PSG nights across multiple suites.
Implement a phased equipment procurement plan that prioritizes high‑yield sensors while deferring lower‑impact additions for future expansion.
levers.review_lever: Planning for scalability can safeguard future growth, but the options overlook the immediate need to balance current throughput with resource constraints.

levers.name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Framework
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will foster cross‑disciplinary knowledge sharing, accelerating methodological innovation. However, increased collaboration may introduce coordination overhead and dilute accountability.
options: Implement a bi‑weekly interdisciplinary roundtable that brings together sleep neurologists, data engineers, and behavioral scientists to review progress.
Implement a shared digital workspace that hosts protocol documents, analysis scripts, and real‑time data dashboards for all team members.
Implement a rotating mentorship program that pairs junior researchers with senior experts across disciplines to build cross‑skill fluency.
levers.review_lever: Encouraging interdisciplinary work can spark innovation, yet the options do not address the risk of fragmented decision‑making when priorities clash.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project must balance high-quality longitudinal data capture with participant safety, operational feasibility, and budget constraints. Core tensions involve maximizing event yield through intensive residential monitoring while preserving a domestic environment to avoid suppressing naturalistic behavior. Lever selection focuses on retention, suite utilization, algorithm validation, infrastructure resilience, funding alignment, and cohort management to navigate speed, cost, scope, and quality trade-offs. These levers directly address the need for reliable data, efficient resource use, and regulatory compliance, ensuring the unit can meet its 36‑month success criteria without compromising safety or scientific rigor.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Improving retention reduces dropout, increasing the likelihood of capturing parasomnia events during the 2–8 week stay. Higher participant satisfaction may lower the average number of weeks needed per enrollee, saving staff overtime costs, but it may also require extended staffing hours to accommodate flexible scheduling.
options: Implement a structured post-discharge follow-up protocol that schedules weekly check-ins, offers flexible rescheduling, and provides personalized sleep diaries.
Introduce a tiered incentive scheme that rewards consecutive nights of compliance, offers bonus meals, and grants early discharge options for high adherence.
Deploy a peer-support network where participants share nightly logs, receive peer feedback, and participate in moderated discussion groups.
levers.review_lever: Balancing participant autonomy with study protocol adherence; the options ignore the potential increase in staff workload to manage peer-support and incentive logistics.

levers.name: Sleep Suite Utilization Optimization
levers.consequences: Optimizing suite occupancy raises the number of nights monitored per week, boosting event yield and reducing idle time. However, it may increase noise and cross-suit contamination risk, requiring stricter hygiene protocols and potentially higher staff oversight.
options: Schedule staggered participant arrivals that align with technician shift transitions, ensuring each suite remains occupied for the maximum feasible duration.
Implement a dynamic suite assignment algorithm that reallocates participants based on real-time event frequency, prioritizing high-activity nights for full PSG deployment.
Introduce a shared suite rotation policy that allows two participants to share a suite during low-risk periods, with strict hygiene protocols and synchronized monitoring.
levers.review_lever: Maximizing throughput versus maintaining data quality and participant safety; the options do not address potential cross-contamination risk or staff burden for hygiene.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Validation
levers.consequences: Validating the semi-automated triage algorithm reduces manual scorer time and speeds data processing. Yet, overly aggressive thresholds could miss rare parasomnia events, affecting the validity of longitudinal phenotyping.
options: Conduct a blinded validation study that compares algorithm-flagged events against dual human scorer adjudication across a representative sample of 200 nights.
Integrate a continuous learning loop that updates algorithm thresholds based on real-time scorer feedback, ensuring adaptive sensitivity over the study period.
Deploy a hybrid triage workflow that combines algorithmic pre-filtering with random human spot-checks, calibrating false-positive rates to a target of 5%.
levers.review_lever: Balancing automation speed with diagnostic accuracy; the options assume the algorithm can be tuned to 5% false positives but ignore potential regulatory constraints on algorithmic decision support.

levers.name: Infrastructure Resilience Planning
levers.consequences: Enhancing power and network redundancy reduces downtime, safeguarding continuous data capture and storage integrity. The upfront capital outlay and ongoing maintenance may strain the €3.8 million budget, especially if upgrades are required mid-project.
options: Install redundant power supplies and UPS systems for all monitoring equipment, ensuring continuous operation during grid outages.
Implement a dual-network architecture that mirrors data streams to an off-site cloud storage, providing instant failover in case of local connectivity loss.
Establish a scheduled quarterly hardware health audit that tests storage integrity, backup verification, and environmental controls across all suites.
levers.review_lever: Upfront resilience investment versus budget constraints; the options overlook potential downtime during audit periods and staff training needs.

levers.name: Funding Milestone Alignment
levers.consequences: Tying disbursements to deliverables enforces accountability and aligns cash flow with progress, but may create pressure to meet milestones at the expense of scientific rigor or lead to cash shortfalls if delays occur.
options: Adopt a milestone-based disbursement schedule that releases 30% of each yearly tranche upon completion of key deliverables such as ethics approval, pilot cohort enrollment, and algorithm prototype.
Introduce a contingency reserve that is automatically replenished when a milestone is delayed, ensuring continuous operations without external funding requests.
Implement a real-time budget monitoring dashboard that flags variance over 10% per month, prompting corrective action before fund depletion.
levers.review_lever: Ensuring financial discipline versus maintaining flexibility for scientific exploration; the options assume smooth milestone tracking but ignore potential delays in regulatory approvals that could stall funding release.

levers.name: Longitudinal Data Cohort Management
levers.consequences: Robust cohort tracking reduces missing data and enhances statistical power for Aim 2, but requires additional staff for data linkage and follow-up, increasing personnel costs.
options: Create a centralized participant registry that records enrollment dates, visit schedules, and event logs, enabling automated reminders for follow-up visits.
Implement a longitudinal data quality audit that flags missing or inconsistent entries within 48 hours, prompting immediate correction by the data engineer.
Deploy a participant engagement portal that provides personalized dashboards, study updates, and secure messaging, fostering long-term adherence to follow-up protocols.
levers.review_lever: Data completeness versus participant burden; the options do not consider the impact of portal usage on data privacy compliance or staff time for support.

levers.name: Recruitment Channel Expansion
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever broadens the pool of potential participants, accelerating enrollment but potentially diluting participant quality due to broader screening criteria. The increased volume may also strain pre‑admission screening resources.
options: Launch a national outreach campaign to recruit from sleep clinics across Germany and surrounding regions.
Establish an online pre‑screening portal to identify potential participants before in‑person visits and reduce initial screening time.
Provide telehealth screening sessions for participants to reduce travel burden and increase enrollment flexibility and engagement.
levers.review: Expanding recruitment channels increases enrollment speed, but the options overlook the risk of diluting participant quality due to broader screening criteria.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but the options neglect that a single hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
